turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear argument j next in 78-733 , Kaiser Aetna, et al., v. the United States. Mr. Boeken, you may proceed whenever you are ready, j OH BEHALF OF THE PETITIONERS barrier3 a beach barrier. There were — and whether they were natural, or not, I don't know3 but an ancient Hawaiian history in the use of the pond as a fish pond, there were sluice gates that x*?ere narrow in nature which did exist when they were placed there — history doesn't tall us, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is way down on the southeast tip of the island, isn't it?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. it is an admitted fact that there was some tidal action in | .4 ' •' _ . there was no evidence —- and I think the government will con­ cede this — there was no traffic between the pond and the sea except I suppose you could physically somewhere carry a small flat bottom boat over the barrier beach and place It in the sea. But certainly three sides it was just land and then the barrier beach which had been there for centuries separated the pond from the ocean.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it was opening up to the sea, that some artificial barriers were placed between the pond and the

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Well, I don’t know, Your Honor, if they would be referred to as artificial barriers,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, were they barriers enough to keep fish in? ponds, the title to them could be transferred. In some cases they were described In land commission awards, some of them, as this pond was. was Included within a parcel of land which was owned by the Bishop Estate, the Bishop Estate being an educational trust created for the supporting of the Hawaii Kassehameha School System, and this particular pond was located within a parcel which was owned and had been owned for a long period by the Bishop Estate, and while owned by the estate it was leased out to various individuals for* use as a fish pond. It was, as 1 mentioned before, not navigable in its natural condition and its private nature had been recognized for centuries, through ancient Hawaiian custom as outlined extensively In our brie? by Hawaii law and by federal law upon annexation of the territory as a state in the union, and that annexation act is codified, which is called the Organic Act, is codified at 48 U.S. Code 506, and it recognised these equivalent of fast land just as a farm or a house lot would be considered. They were not considered separately as part

turns.name: BOOKED
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I think that they would be determined by the state law. I think what would be taken would have to be valued in accordance with state law in the valuation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But how about your concept of riparian servitude which descends from the Commerce Clause regulation of the — Article I of the Constitutions even if state law recognised the- value as a hydroelectric site in the various cases we have decided as a compensable item, if the state were taking under the cases fx'-oia this Court, the federal condemna­ tion would not have to include that in valuation, isn’t that Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In each determination the — in answer to my Brother Rehnquist’s question* whether or not something is property at all depends upon state law. I mean Hawaii law might have been —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That’s correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — that these fish ponds were not the property of the fee owner or the lands surrounding them* and If they had been not property at all then there would be ser© compensation.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That’s correct. I concede that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though the federal government tried

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Oh, yese

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the hydroelectric example given by my brother is one -—

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — or the riparian right in the Rand case is another.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Those are discounted as a matter of federal law. Whether or not the item to be taken is property at all is a matter of state law.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That’s correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Booker*, to go back a little, in the old law and the present law of Hawaii, federal law not being

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: It has been done.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, it could be done legally. That’s all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Bocken, in your view could the Corps of Engineers have blocked off access to the pond without pay­

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Blocked off the access, at what period at this period? 2 property right of a private property owner by some action of the Corps of Engineers, I think the difficulty we have in this case is the seding of water as fast land, and that is what I a:a urging upon this Court,. The petitioners are for a law that treats this pond as fast land, I think if we think of it as fast land, that -- :- QUESTIOM: Until you drain it, it is not? land.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Until you drain it, It is not land.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Maybe I misstate — it Is-'the; legal equivalent of fast land and it is treated as fast; land. It is the treatment of the two.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are saying then that Congress, even exercising its commerce power, could not have provided for public access to this pond?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That’s true, Your Honor, without condemnation and compensation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you think the answer is the same then under the commerce power or under the navigation servitude?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, I think regardless of which this property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you agree then with the court below that you can’t separate the two?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Mo, I disagree with that. I think the federal regulatory authority, if you want to regulate any activities with the pond* you can do that the same as you could with another —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well* how about authorising public access though —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — by statute?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So for that purpose the servitude and the when you are making land or water highways navigable, you do not have to pay certain elements to the landowner.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: And the reason for that being that the navigation servitude always existed on public navigable waters. That 3.3 why the government doesn't have to pay for it, because it always had that right. They didn't have that right in Kuapa Pond, for two reasons: One, because it was not navigable in fact, nor could it be made susceptible to navigability with reasonable resources, but primarily because of Hawaii law which is recognised by the federal —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When did it become navigable?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When did it become navigable?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: It became navigable in fact — and I i .. . area was leased to Kaiser Aetna for development, then it did had a large area that was low, below sealevel. Could he with- j out the approval of the United States Corps of Engineers run a channel from his low land to the sea to let water in to make a navigable —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: No, I think the Corps of Engineers has every right under those circumstances where it might Interfere with navigable waters of the United States, either by clumping waste out into navigable waters, out Into the sea,,■ or causing lv :\V ■ i ■' V:' : i ‘ v . . vi

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Well — QUESTION; This little fish pond had 1,500 marinas. Am I right?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: It has ~~

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, when does it become a big fish pond?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It has got 1,500 marinas in. there now.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: No, Your Honor, it —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how many?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Let me just describe a little bit about --

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: There are slips for — well, there is I am talking about this c&ss.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Mow, this is about 500 acres and It is in its original state -—

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is not a small fish pond.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Mo, no, no, Mo, sirs it was from sero to about

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is a big —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: — at high tide and at low tide the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, who has control of navigation on this little fish pond?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Well, in this pond today the residents pay an assessment for the non-exclusive use of the pond waters. and this assessment pays for a patrol boat which picks up debris, it pays for a patrol boat which tries to manage and to

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, who decides which boat passes to the- right and which beat passes to the left?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Well, they have Internal rules which have been established for the residents, Your Honor, and they are all aware of these and they are printed and the people are

turns.text: The Coast Guard since the Ninth Circuit decision has come in and out occasionally.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But without that —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: But generally speaking, it is an internal control by — eouldn5t you? You could keep the Coast Guard out, couldn’t

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: With the Ninth Circuit decision, you ear51.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Under the Ninth Circuit decision, you could not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I said without the Minth Circuit, you couldn't think as a matter of good comity we could not keep the Coast • ' . ■ • -1 waters and it all becomes navigable — that is the same ease as this? MR, BOCKEN: That * s right. As the Chief Justice said3 if the pond is in Minnesota or Iowa —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The question is whether when somebody does that he has dedicated it to the public or not, that is the —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: No, he does not dedicate it to the public,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that is what we are going to decide.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That is the question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if you reversed the Chief I at all and also for the interior pond that was not navigable at the time?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That’s right, Your Honor, it is all fast land for the purposes of law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, your answer would be the same if the pond were navigable in fact before the canal was build, wouldn’t you?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: That’s true, Your Honor. That’s true, exactly true. However, we have our baek~up position that it wasn’t, just for safety reasons. disputed by petitioners In this case. They have not in any way challenged the government's position that the public has a right stemming from the common law and protected by the Commerce Clause to navigate all navigable waters of the United States * What they do contest is whether Kuapa Pond is a navigable water of the United States* But even as to that question, counsel has just conceded that they don't argue that the pond is not navigable for all purposes. They now concede that it is a navigable water of the United States for purposes of the Corps of Engineers — water but not a navigable water that belongs to the United ; governments authority to regulate. Our position is that the same test of navigability that this Court has developed under the Commerce Clause for purposes of regulatory jurisdiction also determines the public's right of navigation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They both stem from the Commerce Clause.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That’s correct. We believe that the public right to navigate predates the Commerce Clause. It existed before the Constitution was written* but it finds

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if adjacent to this poind on are on the Island of Oahu. Under the commerce power, do they

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: They may have that right under the commerce power, but that doesn't settle the question of whether there has been a compensable taking.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You said they might have to pay.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That's correct, if the government under­ took an activity that was truly on fast land, but our position here is that this fish pond has always both before and after the improvements been a navigable water of the United States and for that reason compensation would not be owed for any­ thing that the government might choose to undertake in the . the whole line of this Court's compensation cases such as Rands dictate that the government does not pay when it ex­ ercises the navigation servitude in those areas. The reason no compensation is owed is not because we are exercising the Commerce Clause power but because we are exercising the navigation servitude.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the navigation servitude depends on the commerce power,

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: It pre-dates the Commerce Clause, al­ though it is protected by the Commerce Clause» but our posi­ tude is not as broad as the government's powers under the of Rands we would not pay any special value for its riparian access. But that is not the situation we have in this case. The government has not taken fast land. The situation we have here is that both before and after the improvements made by Kaiser Aetna, this has always been navigable water of the United States. Before the improvements, just merely by re­ moving the sand bar which separates the bay from the pond, we would have had the navigable waters of the bay flowing into the pond, we could have easily made the pond suitable for use of commerce simply by removing the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was not the Court of Appeals5 theory, : we also argue that it Is subject to the public right of navi­ gation. We agree with petitioners that there are reasons for regulatory powez8 which go beyond navigation, but those are not the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you take the position that if water8 is sufficiently navigable to give the United States regulatory power over it, that it is necessarily subject to servitude?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That’s correct.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: They really aren’t owned by anyone. The United States has paramount authority over them,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. Well,, why isn’t a fact in this case that you are condemning somebody’s land without paying for it?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: We did not condemn --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, suppose you started out to condemn up and gone through condemnation, wouldn’t you?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And for that money you would have gotten ; , : the United States.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what does the United States want done?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: We want two things in this ease. We want them to commence applying for permits under the Rivers and Harbors Act for any future dredging or filling in of the pond or at the connection of the pond to the bay* and we want them to acknowledge that they have made the pond a navigable water of the United States and that it was therefore open for public navigations and that is the important one. And it is only the second question that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that would be free, they couldn’t collect any more money?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: No, because by statute. 33 tf.S.C. , section 565, Congress has provided that private parties may contending that the public has a right to use the privately built slips and docks that comprise maybe the other 23 aeras of the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not if they are navigable waters? far as the Corps of Engineers is aware, this is not even a problem. The Corps is not aware of any examples of public using —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Maybe they don’t want to exercise the power, but as a matter of raw power wouldn’t the United public? right needs to be accommodated with the private interests in ■' '" ■■ j property, protecting navigation, whatever interests he thinks are relevant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then suppose the petitioners here were going to build a hundred additional slips, would they have to get the approval of the Corps of Engineers?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Yes, indeed, and I think they concede that now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they didn't get the permission to build the 500, did they?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: They originally started their activi­ ties in the pond itself before the connection to the bay and at that time, according to an affidavit of their employees, they were advised by the Corps that no permission was needed for work in the pond itself. But beginning in about 1966 or *6?, whan they wanted —- that time on advised them that permits were required. They obtained permits under protest, still contending that they we and it is entirely in Ohio. Would you agree with that?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: And it doesn’t connect to any other navigable waters.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, let’s say that my farm lies both In Ohio and Indiana, which many farms do down wheiae I came from, the southwestern corner of Ohio, and the lake happens to be part of it in Indian and part of it in Ohio. Does that lake automatically belong to the United States?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: If It can be used as a highway for coicmeree -- paramount federal —* . there legally. They can ride around that lake without my per­ mission* without paying me anything* Is that correct?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: If they can get to the lake without crossing your dry land, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Simply because it Is partly in Ohio and partly in Indiana.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That’s correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well* they couldn’t if it was entirely in Ohio? clause.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you concede that when we are looking at the Constitution, the only thing that talks about author­ izing Congress to enforce any right to navigate is the Commerce Clause.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That5 s correct, but the public right pre-dates the Commerce Clause.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what does that mean in terms of constitutional law?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Our position is that the public right came from common law, the coramonese adopted it as their common law, the states had it as their common law, and in the Commerce Clause they surrender to the federal government the power to protect that right against state infringement and —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Couldn’t he sell It before?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Well, now there Is no pond to sell.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So now he can’t sell it now if you get your way? it into navigable waters of the United States.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And voluntarily gave up his right to sell it?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: He does not have to change the pond to the navigable tfaters of the United States —- if he decides to stop —-

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could drain the water out?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: No, but he doesn’t have to •—

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then how could he do it? operations. i

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you don’t know* do you?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: It is 500 acres, deepened from two feet to six feet.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well* you don’t want me to have the job of separating it, do you? Now, suppose he wants to sell his water, he can’t sell it.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That is because he has merged his water with the public’s waters*at no cost to him he has obtained use of the public's waters. Our position is simply that no private person has a right to connect his land or his water to the public’s waters without the government’s permission, anc that as a condition for that permission it is not at all unreasonable to require that the public's waters from the bay which have now been brought onto his pond be kept open to the public.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, even if he didn’t get permission, j you would make the second assertion, if he got no permission ! from anybody, if he had a pond and he dug & navigable ditch to the sea, then under your submission that is open to the public?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Well, he certainly can’t benefit from the fact that he —-

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The answer is yes. Isn’t it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: With or without permission.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That’s right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well., wouldn’t you go further and say if the government had dug the ditch, that the pond would then be open to the public?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Yes, that's connect, and the compensa­ tion question arising out of the government’s digging the ditch would depend upon whether or not the water was always

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: — or whether the government took fast in either event, the government could dredge the pond, the opening and then provide for public access.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought I caught something you said a few moments ago that if the aperture to the sea were sealed off, then this would be restored to the private status with- Interests of navigation, then the government would have the free to return to their fish pond operations and use it as a fish pond.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they are still using that public water that you placed so much reliance on.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Well* we would be cutting off their use of the waters from the bay and they would no longer continue to flow in. I would like to go back for a minute to explain why we believe the Commerce Clause test of navigability is the one that also governs the test for public right of naviga­ tion, and It is simply a matter of common sens®. We believe that the principal reason the govern­ ment’s regulatory power over navigation even exists is to protect the public's right to navigate. It doesn't make much sense to give the Corps of Engineers sweeping powers to pro­ hibit obstruction to navigable waters as Congress has done in the Rivers and Harbors Act if the public Is not entitled been the assumption of Congress In 1899 that the waters it was directing the Secretary of War to keep free of obstruc­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then doesn't It follow that the putlie can get in the slips?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: No, we are willing to concede that the public right is subject to reasonable regulations. One theory would be that the Cor-ps of Engineers9 permit to the slip owner to build implies a right of exclusive use of the slip and: mooring area there. The permit does not run to the gen­ it to be a meaningful permit he has to be able to find his parking space open when he gets there. Another thing would be the Corps could set regula­ tions under the authority of section 1 of Title 33 that would 1 •* ' .i type of regulation, we would also agree that it is the type regulation. Again, it would not be an impediment to naviga­ tion but it would, rather foster navigation by keeping the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wells there might be an argument about it and in any event that would be a matter of policy and not of power.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: That's correct, but our position here is that the petitioners have never asked the Corps of Engineers to set reasonable limits on the public's use of the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't see the difference logically be­ tween saying an owner of a slip can control the slip and an what you have got here, is fifty 3lips or 500, whatever the ' -4 ■i ;U ..

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: No, and we are not asking that the public be able to us® either one or 600 of the slips. He are talking about the approximately 500 acres of open water, and that is all that the government?s position is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose the open water were useful only for parking 600 boats, you would still have legally the same position, wouldn’t you?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: If it were so small that that was all, that you would only have room for 600 slips, that might be a different case. But her® we have a pond that is over 500 acres, it is two miles long, it serves as access to the Pacific Ocean and we simply don’t have that limited case before the Court now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the access to the Pacific Ocean is both what gives you the power and also what makes it as a reason for the development, both, FIS. OBERLY: And petitioners the reason for the M3. OBEF.LY: But they had no private entitlement to use the public*s waters in the bay and the ocean, and that is what they have done here. Their result basically sanctions brought onto their land. This Court has held in United States is inconceivable and yet that is what petitioners are to build a marina and to have 400 slips., that wouldn’t ~~

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: — and the permit does not run to the general public.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. But the water would9 Just like yon are contending here that it does. MS. 9BERLT: The open waters d©8 yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let’s extend that a little. Suppose a class action Is brought by some nearby yacht eiub* a group of boat owners and they insist on the right to build a hundred slips in there for their own convenience. Do you think the federal authority can permit them to build a hundred slips?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Well, the federal authority can pro­ hibit their building the slips in the first place. They need •:

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All righta then, the Corps exercises the discretion and says we can build a hundred slips. To what do they attach the slips?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: The probably can't because they don't have the riparian ownership of the surrounding land and for the Corps to grant a permit in those circumstances would think it is unlikely that the Corps would authorise building

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but that issue Isn’t herea I guess, as to whether the Coast Guard could or couldn’t establish the rules of the road in the pond?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: They do concede that the Corps of Engineers has regulatory jurisdiction. I would take that to include a concession that the Coast Guard can establish the rules of the road, but in fact they are establishing their own rules of the road for this pond. pots and seine fishing, for example? doesn't make any difference which test applies„ it is still navigable?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: Vie are happy with the Court's tradi­ tional test 3 which is navigability in fact- at any point in time, either in the past or present or in the future with reasonable — flow test was the traditional one. hiris elf without —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He can't close it off?

turns.name: KATHRYN A. OBERLY
turns.text: No. The final point is petitioners' reliance on Hawaii law. We have explained in our briefs why we think Hawaiian law does not require the result they are arguing fors but even if it did the question for this Court is simply whether Congress meant to adopt that rule in the Hawaii Organic Act, and again our brief adequately explains that Congress was only it was not dealing with the public right of navigation. So there is no reason for assuming that Congress intended to forfeit the public right based on an act dealing with fishing ponds when today we have no fish pond in existence any more. AFTERNOON SESSION — 1:00 O’CLOCK

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Boeken, you have about six minutes.

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor. We would just like to clarify a few things and bring home a few points. One is that there has been reference made to 525 acres of pond, a body of water consisting of that and that simply isn’t true* Your Honors. I think the record will reflect that it is considerably less than that. There are islands, there was much dredging done and residential islands made throughout there, so the pond —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For the purposes of this case., does it . think the record will cl rify th t. As to the permit ctivity , I do not w nt to h ve the Court led to ny misconception th t Bishop Est te nd K iser were somehow slipping through nd cre ting things without coordin tion with the Corps. The Corps w s fully w re t ll times of the ctivities in the pond. In f ct, when the pro­ pos l w s being m de to build bridge which required some K iser Aetn wrote to the Corps of Engineers nd dvised them dredging on the oce n side, nd th t permit w s gr nted. And • interestingly enough, the defend nt’s Exhibit 15 in our joint ppendix st tes th t — in this f shion -- it is ddressed to ..

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: For Kaiser Aetna.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As to the total dredging process» the total development of this lake —

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The total developmental projects was he the project director — MR. BGCKE?^: He was the project director for Kaiser Aetna at that time, Your Honor9 right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well» what do you get from that letter? Does that letter bind the United States government in any form or fashion?

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: I think I am pointing this out to the

turns.name: RICHARD CHARLES BOCKEN
turns.text: We affirmatively deny that it was

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is a letter to whom? MR, BOCKEN: This is a letter to the Corps of Engineers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does that bind the federal government? MR. 80CKEN: It does ~ I can't argue estoppel, Your Honor. I am not saying that estoppel applies in this situation. But I am arguing that certainly it indicates the/fc we didn't intend to dedicate the pond to public use. That was never our intention, never the petitioners' inten­ \ ' conclude with this thought: If Kuapa Pond is burdened with the public navigation servitude, as the government urges, there will be, one, a public confiscation of private property for public recreational use of an area that was conceived, built, paid for and maintained by private funds, and confiscation would not only be servitude of the waters but the use of all of these improvements to which I just made mention. And, two, such a result is constitutionally pro­ scribed by the Fifth Amendment. It is not justified by any legitimate public interest and reeks of inequity.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Your time is expired now, Mr. BOcken. above-entitled matter was submitted.)
